title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: digital storytelling platforms and tools, community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy in small towns, rural cultural sector innovation, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, preservation of rural cultural heritage through technology, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. Prefer articles that bridge culture AND technology in rural/Cariboo contexts. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Resilience.org] Understanding how plants pause and restart growth can help develop climate‑resilient crops
items.content_html: New research shows that plants can temporarily halt root growth under stresses like cold and drought, then rapidly restart it once conditions improve. By identifying the genes behind this “pause and push” response, scientists hope to develop crops that recover more quickly from extreme weather, strengthening food security in a changing climate.

items.title: [Engadget] Rivian begins production on the R2 electric SUV
items.content_html:
Rivian has begun production of its R2 SUV. However, you can't get one just yet: The first customer deliveries (of the most expensive version) aren't expected until later this spring.

On Wednesday, CEO RJ Scaringe drove the first electric SUV off the production line at the company's Normal, IL, factory. A storage and logistics building at that factory was damaged by a tornado last weekend, with Wednesday's rollout event seemingly designed to reassure nervous customers and investors.

"We are really excited to be producing R2 for our customers," Scaringe is quoted as saying in a news release. However, Rivian CFO Claire McDonough told Reuters that customers won't be able to configure their vehicle orders until June. Electrek reports that these first units rolling out now are going to Rivian employees.

If you were drawn to the R2's $45,000 starting price, well, Rivian won't have any of those for a while. First off the line (this spring) is the Launch Package, starting at $57,990. A Premium trim, expected late 2026, will cost $53,990. Then, in the first half of 2027, a Standard (RWD long range) variant arrives at $48,490. And as for that headline-grabbing $45,000 base-model R2, I hope you like waiting. It won't be here until late 2027.

The Rivian R2 was revealed in 2024. Smaller and lighter than the flagship R1, the company is positioning the EV as its answer to Tesla’s best-selling Model Y. All versions of the new two-row SUV are rated for at least 300 miles per charge. Each trim has a native NACS charge port. The vehicle can charge from 10 percent to 80 percent in under 30 minutes when using a DC fast charger.

This article originally appeared on Engadget at https://www.engadget.com/transportation/evs/rivian-begins-production-on-the-r2-electric-suv-171729320.html?src=rss

items.title: [New Atlas] AI neckband lets you talk without saying a word
items.content_html:
Scientists at Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), in South Korea, have built a silicone neckband that reads the tiny movements of your neck as you mouth words – and turns them into speech in your own voice, transmitted to whoever is listening.
